programs.name: Four Pillar Training Program
programs.description: An evidence-based training system built around four pillars — Strength, Endurance, Mobility, and Healthspan. Every exercise and protocol is selected from peer-reviewed research with the goal of keeping you strong, mobile, and capable for life. Not a bodybuilder program. A longevity program.

citations.principle: VO₂ max is the strongest predictor of all-cause mortality — no upper bound of benefit
citations.keyFinding: Low-fit men had 5.04× mortality vs. elite-fit; stronger predictor than smoking or diabetes

citations.principle: Each 1-MET VO₂ max increase = 13–15% lower all-cause mortality
citations.keyFinding: n=750,302 US veterans; linear association, no plateau

citations.principle: Norwegian 4×4 protocol produces 7–13% VO₂ max gain in 8 weeks
citations.keyFinding: 4×4 outperformed all other interval protocols tested

citations.principle: Zone 2 drives mitochondrial biogenesis and fat oxidation via MCT1
citations.keyFinding: Lactate shuttle theory; Zone 2 uniquely targets Type I mitochondria

citations.principle: Lifelong strength training preserves Type II fiber proportion vs. endurance or untrained
citations.keyFinding: Strength-trained masters: 52% Type II fibers; recreationally active: 39.3%

citations.principle: Hypertrophy equal across load spectrum when taken to failure; strength favors heavy load
citations.keyFinding: 21 RCTs; high load ~2× more effective for 1RM strength

citations.principle: Minimum effective dose: 4 sets/muscle/week for growth; 1 set/week for maintenance
citations.keyFinding: Dose-response; diminishing returns pronounced past ~15 sets/week for strength

citations.principle: Each 5 kg lower grip strength = 16% higher all-cause mortality, 17% cardiovascular mortality
citations.keyFinding: n=139,691 across 17 countries; grip stronger predictor than systolic BP

citations.principle: Heavy resistance training (≥85% 1RM) significantly improved BMD; low-intensity did not
citations.keyFinding: 5×5 deadlift + squat + OHP; significant lumbar + femoral neck BMD gains

citations.principle: Nordic curl inclusion halves hamstring injury rates
citations.keyFinding: n=8,459 athletes; 51% reduction in hamstring strain risk

citations.principle: Trap-bar deadlift: ~10% lower spinal moment, higher power vs. conventional
citations.keyFinding: 19 powerlifters; trap bar allows higher loads at lower joint stress

citations.principle: Unilateral training matches bilateral for strength; outperforms for speed + agility
citations.keyFinding: Bulgarian split squat group superior on sprint + change-of-direction tests

citations.principle: Concurrent training causes negligible hypertrophy or strength loss when programmed correctly
citations.keyFinding: 43 studies; interference effect mainly in explosive strength, not hypertrophy

exercises.notes: Rear foot elevated

exercises.name: McGill Big 3
exercises.notes: Bird Dog · Side Plank · Modified Curl-Up

exercises.name: Bird Dog
exercises.notes: Opposite arm and leg. Brace before moving. Straight line from heel to fingertips. Hold 2–3 s at top.

exercises.name: Side Plank
exercises.notes: Stack feet or stagger for an easier variation. Keep hips high — don't let them sag.

exercises.name: Modified Curl-Up
exercises.notes: Hands under lumbar spine to maintain natural curve. Lift head and shoulders only — not a full crunch. Brief hold at top.

exercises.notes: Full scapular retraction

exercises.name: Norwegian 4×4 VO₂ Max Intervals

mobilityRoutines.name: Morning Routine
mobilityRoutines.notes: Do before coffee. Daily consistency beats occasional long sessions.

exercises.name: Hip CARs
exercises.notes: Full hip circle — signals nervous system you own full ROM

exercises.name: Shoulder CARs
exercises.notes: Separates scapula from humeral motion — critical for climbers

exercises.name: Thoracic Spine CARs
exercises.notes: Desk work kills T-spine extension; opens breathing and posture

exercises.name: Chin Tucks
exercises.notes: Activates deep neck flexors; reverses upper crossed syndrome

exercises.name: Band Pull-Aparts
exercises.notes: Highest-leverage desk-worker corrective

exercises.name: Glute Bridge
exercises.notes: Wakes glutes after sitting; prevents anterior pelvic tilt

mobilityRoutines.name: Desk Worker Micro-Habits
mobilityRoutines.notes: Set a timer. These compound over a full work day into meaningful volume.

exercises.name: Desk Hip Flexor Stretch
exercises.notes: Counteracts hip flexor shortening from sitting

exercises.name: Chin Tuck + Neck Roll
exercises.notes: 1 inch forward head = 10 lb extra spinal load

exercises.name: Thoracic Rotation in Chair
exercises.notes: Do while on a call

exercises.name: Standing Glute Squeeze
exercises.notes: Prevents glute amnesia — key driver of lower back pain

exercises.name: Wrist Extension Stretch
exercises.notes: Critical for climbers — prevents flexor overuse imbalance

mobilityRoutines.name: Lower Body RAMP
mobilityRoutines.notes: Run before any lower body session.

exercises.name: Light Cardio
exercises.notes: Bike, row, or walk — just get warm

exercises.name: Leg Swings (front-back + lateral)
exercises.notes: Neural warm-up for hip flexors and adductors

exercises.name: World's Greatest Stretch
exercises.notes: Best single-movement for lower-body prep

exercises.name: Ankle Dorsiflexion Wall Drill
exercises.notes: DF restriction accounts for up to 43% of squat depth variance

mobilityRoutines.name: Upper Body RAMP
mobilityRoutines.notes: Run before any upper body or climbing session.

exercises.name: Band Pull-Aparts
exercises.notes: Activates rear delts and scapular retractors before pressing

exercises.name: Scap Push-Ups
exercises.notes: Activates serratus — most neglected UCS corrective muscle

exercises.name: Doorway Pec Stretch
exercises.notes: Opens thorax; prevents shoulder impingement from climbing

mobilityRoutines.name: Post-Workout Cooldown
mobilityRoutines.notes: 3×/week minimum. Converts passive ROM to active usable range.

exercises.name: Couch Stretch
exercises.notes: Best hip flexor release after heavy split squats

exercises.name: 90/90 Hip Switches
exercises.notes: Converts passive hip mobility to active usable range

exercises.name: Pigeon Pose / Figure-4
exercises.notes: Complements climbing which tends to use IR dominantly

exercises.name: Foam Roller T-Spine Extensions

exercises.name: Dead Hang

exercises.name: Jefferson Curl

benchmarks.name: Trap-Bar Deadlift
benchmarks.description: Best set weight across any rep range. Target is your 5RM.

benchmarks.name: Dead Hang
benchmarks.description: Continuous timed hang from pull-up bar.

benchmarks.name: Farmer's Carry
benchmarks.description: Bodyweight per hand × 1 minute. Uses best carry weight from workout log.

benchmarks.name: Weighted Pull-Up
benchmarks.description: Best additional weight used for any rep.

benchmarks.name: VO₂ Max
benchmarks.description: Estimated from wearable or lab test. Target top 25th percentile for age.

exercises.notes: Heaviest compound of the week. Prioritize form.
Rear foot on bench. At high load rivals barbell squat for quad + glute stimulus.
Hip hinge, not squat. Drive hips through aggressively.
Anchor feet under bench or partner. Slow negative is the stimulus.

exercises.load: Heaviest DBs — grip is the limiter
exercises.notes: Grip, traps, core, posture. Targets Centenarian Decathlon carry benchmark.
Non-negotiable. Hold 2–3 s at top each rep.
Target 20–25 min. Bike or incline walk. Counts toward 150 min/week Zone 2 target.
Best upper-body pull indicator for longevity (grip + back strength). Add weight when you hit 6 reps.
Neutral grip. Safer to push solo than barbell. Full ROM — touch chest.
Chest on incline bench removes lower back from the equation.
Arc-friendly shoulder press. Lower impingement risk than vertical press.
External rotation + horizontal abduction. Essential for climbers.
Decompresses spine, trains grip, builds scap stability. Log your time.
Bike or easy row after lifting.
Counts as upper-body pulling volume for today. Replaces — not supplements — a separate pull session.
Balances the climbing pulling volume.
Essential for climbers. Carry a band in your chalk bag.
Extensor balance for climbing flexors.
5 min bike or row + dynamic stretches
Bike, rower, or incline treadmill. You should not be able to speak during the 4 min intervals. 3 min recovery at easy pace between each.
Lower knee compressive force vs. back squat. Good for long-term knee health.
Hip stability and left-right asymmetry correction.
Anti-lateral-flexion core demand.
3-day plan uses 3 sets vs 4 to leave energy for the rest of the session.
Hold 2–3 s at top each rep.
Replaces upper-body pull day entirely. If no climbing this week, substitute Wednesday Upper Body from 4-day plan.

workouts.sessionType: Upper Body Push + Pull + VO₂ Max (alternating)

exercises.notes: Do BEFORE lifting on Week A.
Do AFTER lifting on Week B.

plans.name: Home Plan
plans.scheduleDescription: Home Day A · Home Day B · Climbing Gym (Sat)  |  Any 3 days that work

exercises.notes: Full hip circle — primes the joint before loading.
Sit in 90/90, lean forward on front shin. Clears internal rotation before loading.
Bridge up, hold, alternately lift knees. Activates glutes before hinge work.
Primary hinge at home. Hip hinge from hips, soft knees, DBs track close to legs. Go as heavy as form allows.
Free-standing, slight knee bend. Trains hip stability and corrects left-right asymmetry.
Adds posterior-chain volume with zero axial load. Good if fatigued from RDLs.
Rear foot on bench. At this load rivals barbell squat for quad + glute stimulus at roughly half the spinal load.
Hold one DB at chest, sit deep. Reinforces squat pattern and adds quad volume.
Drive through heel, stand tall at top. Extra unilateral quad volume.
Grip should be the limiting factor. Targets Centenarian Decathlon carry benchmark.
Top foot on bench, bottom foot off floor. Best adductor + lateral core exercise at home.
Hold 2–3 s at top each rep.
Off step edge. Slow tempo is the point for Achilles and patellar tendon health.
Couch stretch alternative — best hip flexor release after split squats.
90/90 hip switches — converts passive hip mobility to active usable range.
Supine hamstring stretch. Drop if short on time.
Full shoulder circle — critical joint prep before pressing and pulling.
Activates rear delts and scapular retractors before pressing.
Activates serratus anterior and trains upward rotation — counters desk posture.

groups.name: HORIZONTAL PRESS — replaces Neutral-Grip DB Bench + Landmine Press

exercises.notes: Full ROM — touch chest, full lockout. Safer to push hard solo than a barbell: can dump DBs safely.

exercises.load: BW + load via belt or DB between feet
exercises.notes: One of the best pressing movements available at home. Shoulders below elbows at the bottom.
Upper arms contact floor each rep — removes leg drive, isolates chest/triceps.
Seated for ceiling clearance. Overhead strength and shoulder stability. Replaces landmine press.

groups.name: PULL — replaces Weighted Pull-Up + Chest-Supported Row

exercises.notes: Brace opposite hand on bench. Elbow drives back and up. Primary pull at home.
Rings at hip height, body horizontal. Steeper = harder. Best horizontal pull at home. Weather-dependent.
Face-down on incline bench. No lower-back fatigue. Good when rings aren't available.
Anchor at eye height, pull to face with elbows high and wide. Best exercise for rotator cuff + rear delt health.
Elbow tucked, forearm rotates out. Protects external rotators that climbing neglects.
Decompress spine, maintain grip and shoulder health. Progress toward 2-min continuous hang benchmark.
Medial delt — supports overhead stability. Quick to do.
Arm at 90° on doorframe, rotate away. Opens anterior shoulder — essential after pressing.
Walk hands to one side to bias the lat. Climber staple — keeps lats long.
Side-lying, rotate top shoulder toward floor. Decompresses thoracic spine.
Primary reason to be here. Keep progressing — home Bulgarian split squats are maintenance; this is progression.
Home RDLs preserve the pattern; this is where you push weight. Reset fully between reps.
With safeties + rack you can push harder than at home. Your heavy press day.
The one bilateral row where you can load beyond what DBs allow. Mid-back in a way DB rows can't fully replicate.
This IS your Zone 2 + VO₂ stimulus + grip volume + upper pulling for the week. Don't skip climbing for more barbell work.
Heavier than home. Home carries cover maintenance; this pushes the Centenarian Decathlon benchmark.

programs.releaseNotes: Cardio now shows a target zone band on the live bar (Norwegian 4×4 → Z4–Z5). Dedicated Zone 2 blocks hold the countdown until you reach Zone 2 (tap Start Now to skip) so the prescribed time is time *in* zone.
